1: hoy hasha'ananim b'Tzion — "Woe to those who are complacent in Zion." Sha'ananim (at ease, complacent, undisturbed) describes those whose tranquility is groundless self-confidence. The woe formula (hoy) marks a funeral lament directed at the living — the recipients are being mourned before their death arrives. Zion and Samaria are addressed together, the twin capitals of divided Israel, their residents imagined as "notable men of the first of the nations" — a bitter irony, since status in human ranking counts for nothing in the coming reversal.
2: Calneh, Hamath, Gath — Amos summons Israel to survey the great cities that have fallen: Calneh in Assyria, Hamath on the Orontes, Gath of the Philistines. The rhetorical question "Are they better than these kingdoms?" points to conquered territories as the destiny awaiting the complacent. The cities named were major powers within living memory — their downfall provides a warning Israel is refusing to read.
3: ha-menaddim l'yom ra — "those who put off the day of disaster." The participle menaddim suggests a pushing-away or postponing — the complacent actively suppress awareness of coming judgment. Meanwhile they "bring near a seat of violence," the very thing they pretend is far off. The inversion — distancing the day of reckoning while drawing violence near — captures the self-defeating logic of complacency.
4: ha-shokevim al mitot shein — "those who lie on beds of ivory." Archaeological finds at Samaria confirm ivory-inlaid furniture among the elite. The participles in Amos 6:4-6 accumulate to build a portrait of sensory indulgence: lying, sprawling, eating lambs from the flock, eating calves from the stall — animals chosen for their tenderness, animals that belong to others. The food imagery is abundance stripped of gratitude.
5: ha-poretin al pi ha-nevel — "those who improvise to the sound of the lyre." Poretin (improvising, strumming idly) has a pejorative edge — the music is self-entertainment without purpose. The comparison to David is audacious: David played before YHWH in lament and praise; these players invent instruments for self-pleasure. The same cultural material (music, instruments) serves opposite ends depending on orientation.
6: ha-shotim b'mizraqei yain — "those who drink wine in bowls (mizraqim)." Mizraq normally designates the sacrificial basin used in temple ritual — drinking from such vessels suggests the sacred has been domesticated as luxury. Anointing with the finest oils compounds the sensory portrait. The indictment's climax: "they are not grieved over the ruin of Joseph" (shever Yosef) — the covenant community is collapsing and the elite feel nothing. Shever means fracture, destruction — the exact word used for the destruction Amos announces.
7: laken ata yiglu b'rosh golim — "therefore they shall now be the first to go into exile." The irony is precise: those who claimed to be "the first of the nations" (Amos 6:1) will be the first to go into exile. The verb galah (go into exile, uncover) is the covenant punishment for covenant violation. The "revelry of those who sprawl" (mirzah ha-seruhim) will end — mirzah is a cultic feast associated with the dead, a term of association found in Ugaritic texts as well, suggesting a quasi-cultic character to these gatherings.
8: nishba Adonai bi-nafshō — "the Lord YHWH swears by himself." When YHWH swears by himself (nishba bi-nafshō, literally "by his soul/being"), the oath is absolute — there is no higher authority by which to guarantee it. The object of divine loathing is ge'on Yaakov ("the pride of Jacob") — the same pride elsewhere celebrated as divine gift has become, through self-congratulatory complacency, the thing YHWH finds abhorrent.
9: im yivateru asara anashim — "if ten men remain in a single house, they shall die." The decimation scenario underscores totality of judgment — even small surviving clusters will not escape. The verse prepares for the strange scene in Amos 6:10, where survivors are afraid even to mention the name of YHWH in connection with the dead.
10: al tazker b'shem YHWH — "do not invoke the name of YHWH." When a kinsman comes to carry out a corpse and asks if others are present, the survivor says "No" — and adds, "Silence! We must not invoke the name of YHWH." The motive is terror: the divine name, which in times of blessing is the source of protection, has become in times of judgment an additional danger to invoke. The reversal of the name's function signals total covenant breakdown.
11: ki hineh YHWH metzaveh — "for YHWH is commanding." The present participle metzaveh gives the judgment a quality of immediacy — not future but already in process. The great house will be struck into rubble (breaches, reshim) and the small house into splinters. The completeness of the destruction makes no distinction between wealth levels: both great and small houses are equally vulnerable when the divine command goes forth.
12: hayerutzun ba-sela susim — "Can horses gallop on bare rock?" The rhetorical questions establish logical impossibility: horses cannot gallop on rock, one cannot plow rock with oxen. Yet Israel has done something equally impossible and self-defeating — turning mishpat (justice, the grain that sustains life) into rosh (poison/bitter herb) and the fruit of tzedaqah (righteousness) into laanah (wormwood). The perversion of justice defies the same logic as perverting nature.
13: ha-simechim l'Lo-davar — "those who rejoice in Lo-dabar." Lo-dabar means "nothing, no-thing" — a town east of the Jordan that Israel treats as a military achievement. Qarnayim ("two horns") likewise sounds like a military trophy, but qarnayim also means horns of an animal — symbols of strength they have acquired by "our own strength." The boast is double-edged: the places of supposed triumph are wordplays on nothing and animal strength, undermining the confidence they inspire.
14: ki hineni meqim alechem goy — "for I am raising up a nation against you." The participle again signals immediacy. From the entrance of Hamath to the Brook of the Arabah describes the full extent of the land — what the nation shall oppress is not a portion of Israel but its entirety. The nation is unnamed (Assyria), which heightens the threat: the identity of the instrument matters less than the certainty of the divine agency behind it.
